sentences.text: [INAUDIBLE].
Right.
Chuma has survived, and she's in Toronto.
Till 1948, till we immigrated to Canada.
Me, my wife, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because my brother came before.
He came January 16, 1948.
In Bergen-Belsen.
Lily.
Weiss.
Hm.
That was exactly the day I came back from Paris, and I met her near the swimming pool.
And so it did go on.
Correct
Yeah.
Yes, there were social activities.
There were also like sport and schools for children.
And were theaters, movies.
There actually was one building, but they tried-- so they joined the Jewish agency, they tried to provide us with activities.
Correct.
What things would you do together?
Oh, well, sometime we went like to an opera, we went to dance, being in the Bergen-Belsen, because it was organized there.
It was-- it was a good life, and it was not so good, because people didn't know what to do.
A lot of people did wait to immigrate.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] That's all.
Where were you married?
We were married in Celle in Germany in the City Hall.
And then we came over here.
We had the another [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jewish-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not with the people but just we went to the rabbi.
No, there was no reason.
The only thing that had come out the immigration so fast.
So we-- because my wife had papers to go to Sweden.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and she fell in love with me, and I fell in love with her.
And we had to make it fast, because we had to give it to the immigration, the papers.
Eh, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] June the 10th.
To Canada, Toronto.
That was the first-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the first-- I remember at night was Rosh Hashanah.
I think it was the 1st of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] November [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] or September or November, something like--
1948. 1st of September.
We arrived to Halifax.
We came by the boat, General [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And from Halifax we came to Toronto.
And my brother, he did wait on the station for us.
That's all.
We very happy that we are in Toronto.
Tailoring.
I started to work on [INAUDIBLE] at the [INAUDIBLE].
Yes.
Two.
Sally and Tomy.
Yes, happily married.
Bentley.
Yes.
We have two grandchildren, Jessica and Shoshanah.
Yes.
I beg your pardon?
Yes, I'm still working a little bit.
Landsmannschaft, yes.
The Tomaszow Society.
Oh, yeah, with people from Tomaszow, yeah, very much so.
Yes, that's correct.
The first trial was in 1970.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was near Frankfurt, a suburb from Frankfurt.
I forgot the city.
The trial was against Fuchs, Boettich and Reichler.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The was the first trial.
Boettich was a Gestapo.
Fuchs and Reichler, they were SS.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Boettich was a volksdeutsche.
That means a Polish-German.
Fuchs was also a volksdeutsche Polish-German, and Reichler was from Austria.
The contact-- I didn't have a special contact, but I did see him, and I did see what they did, the Reichler and Fuchs, they were the main killers of Jews-- the main.
In Tomaszow.
Just take the gun and killed, just like this.
That Fuchs was-- everybody, not only me, everybody did see it.
They didn't hide from anybody.
In front of your eyes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Reichler had a partner too.
His name was Olch.
I didn't see him on the trial.
All of these walked around with two dogs.
Ooh.
In the ghetto.
The German-- German government.
They did come here to look for witnesses, not only me, a lot of people from Tomaszow, the went on trial-- on this trial.
Eh, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as far as I know, they did get some kind of sentence.
I don't know how many years or whatever it is.
But Fuchs and Reichler, they got sentence.
Boettich, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he didn't get such a big sentence, I think.
What, the Fuchs and Reichler?
For sure.
Boettich?
Boettich, I knew, because when we did play soccer, he did play first in the Victoria German team, and then he did play at the [INAUDIBLE].
So that's where I knew him.
He knew me too.
Yes.
I've been in Hamburg twice [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for the lagerfuhrer from Blizyn.
His name is Nell.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And apparently he got life sentence.
The only thing I remember, he didn't talk at all.
But during the trial, I asked the judge through a dolmetscher-- interpreter-- or I can ask him a question, and they, sure, any question you want, you can ask him.
I ask him, did anybody in the camp receive a fair trial like he did-- like you do?
There was no comment.
He didn't-- not a word.
He didn't speak not a word during the whole trial.
He was-- before he went into the courtroom, he was free.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I recognized him right away.
I said to my wife, I said, that's him.
Oh, well, they brought over pictures here, were, you know, some judges and the prosecutors from the German court.
And what I witnessed by him, that he was-- I didn't see him killing himself, but he did give orders.
I remember two people from Piotrkow, they did escape Blizyn, and they catch them.
And they made an appeal, and everybody had to watch the way they've been shot.
So two Ukrainians, they shot.
They could have done white gloves, and they had to knee, and they went over right here and just like this.
One, as even on the trial, they ask me.
I remember even the name from the Ukraine.
One-- one was Moskolenko.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And he gave the orders.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He gave the orders, not only him, to shoot some other people too.
Well, to my opinion, very, very nice.
Very, very nice.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can't say anything about it.
Maybe some people, which always they were not so happy.
We were happy.
I-- I think it's very, very important for the future generations and probably for my grandchildren, as one time my granddaughter asked me, Zayde, what is that number?
She said, it's your phone number?
And I didn't talk to them about this.
But later on, they talked to me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I answered them, special last year.
We were in Poland.
I took my son-in-law, my granddaughter to Birkenau, and they did see, they witnessed, which they wanted to go.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's important for them to know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I would say nothing, because we live.
We didn't think of the future.
We didn't know what's going to be tomorrow.
You live day by day, hour by hour.
The only thing we knew, one of these days will be finished.
This we knew.
Plain luck.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's what I think, plain luck.
That's all.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't do any miracle.
Kindness to each other?
Yes.
Or for from Poles or anybody else.
I always-- I am that kind of person.
But sometimes it's no good, the way I am.
I know myself.
No, this-- yeah, I one Polish guy, yes.
He's in Texas now.
As a matter fact, he is from Warsaw.
And he was always with us after the liberation.
I remember when I went to look for my sister, we went to Poland.
He went too.
And he went to Warsaw, and he was stitched to be anti-Semitic.
He told us.
His name, Wacek Malinowski.
And he went to Warsaw.
He had a sister and a mother in Warsaw.
And he said, how could you do a thing to me like this and teach me against Jews?
I live with Jews.
My name is David, and the whole families and-- that's what he-- and he said, I'm going.
I don't want to come anymore.
And it's a matter of fact, he married a Jewish girl too.
And where was it that you had contact with him?
In Ohrdruf.
He was a cook, and I was a cook.
And he was the guy, after they threw out all the Jews, he helped me.
He would give me bread every day, soup.
Everybody who did work in the kitchen had-- had a chance to do this-- if he wants to.
Have you had contact with him since the end of the war?
We've been all the time, all the years in Bergen-Belsen, and since he went to Texas, I never been in contact with him.
I don't know why, and I ask so many times-- many, many times.
Apparently, he passed away.
So one friend told me, but he lives in Texas-- in Houston, TX.
No, I didn't change.
I'm the same thing.
I believe in my faith.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Not at all.
I believe I'm Jewish.
I'm proud to be Jewish.
Yes, I do.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do.
The first thing something you say, oh, my god.
I don't know.
That's the way I was brought up.
Yeah.
Well, you always think God helps you-- you always think.
Or it's true or it's not true, but that's the way.
By surviving?
I don't know.
I would say it's luck.
I have no idea how to describe this.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
They should know, which a lot of people, they learn, and they knew.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The only thing, I hope it would never ever happen.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Let's hope.
Plenty.
That somebody go after me, the same Meister Pichler, with the gun.
He wants to kill me, and I escape, and I start to scream, and I scream. and my wife, she came up, she slapped me in the face, oh, a few times.
If she would be here, she would tell you.
And apparently such a terrible scream.
It's not a normal screen, she said.
A long time I didn't have any now.
But still sometime.
Oh, did I have those dreams.
Yes.
Sometime.
Sometime.
But my brother, he passed away, but my sister, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she can't hear about Poland.
She-- her husband, they went away to Europe.
Her husband is from Warsaw.
He said let's go.
She said for no money in the world.
You just put down $100 million, she wouldn't go.
She wouldn't go.
Do you think your survival had anything to do with the fact that you and your brother were together?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, we did stick together, as plain as this.
That's all.
Yes.
Yes.
Yeah, I remember he stares, sometime would come in his eyes, when I did work in the kitchen in Ohrdruf, and he did come over after the shift and look at me into my room and the way I looked.
I looked so nice and not nourished and so you understand.
And he did-- he was the happiest man.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Very good.
The relationship was very good.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Before the war and after the war.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No.
No, not at.
I'm the-- I'm the same person.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No.
No, because, to be honest with you, we didn't talk so much.
But lately, we've talked about.
In the beginning, we didn't.
Maybe it wasn't right.
Because they start to growing, and they went to school, and they are learning something.
So that's the reason they ask me questions.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like the little one, Shoshanah, said Zayde, when you were in camp, what did you do?
I said I had to work.
And she said, if you didn't want to work.
I said they could shoot me.
They could kill me.
And you know what she mentioned to me?
She said I rather they shoot me and I go to heaven [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and not to do what they want.
Eh, 11 years, she will be.
Yes, I would like to.
The only thing is my wish [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [SIGHS] that my grandchildren and all the children in the world that don't have to go through that experience what I went through.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And be good to each other.
[PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS] That's a [INAUDIBLE].
[SIGHS]
You're welcome.
